edges.description: Aberrant ATP release from mutant hemichannels disrupts normal keratinocyte differentiation and desquamation.
edges.source: Aberrant Hemichannel Activity and ATP Release

edges.description: Reduced gap junction-mediated intercellular communication contributes to impaired keratinocyte differentiation and hyperkeratosis.
Cx30 dysfunction can also affect gap-junction-dependent inner-ear physiology in the subset with hearing impairment.
Impaired keratinocyte differentiation and desquamation produce palmoplantar hyperkeratosis.
Generalized epidermal differentiation defects can manifest as follicular hyperkeratotic papules.
Keratoderma-associated epidermal disruption can include joint-predominant hyperpigmentation.
Hair-follicle gap-junction dysfunction produces progressive scalp and body hair loss.
Hair-follicle involvement can affect eyebrow hair as part of the broader hypotrichosis phenotype.
Hair-follicle involvement can affect eyelashes as part of the broader hypotrichosis phenotype.
Disrupted nail matrix gap-junction signaling impairs nail plate formation and produces dystrophic nails.
Inner-ear Cx30 dysfunction can manifest as sensorineural hearing loss in a minority of affected individuals.

nodes.description: The G11R and A88V mutations in Cx30 confer a gain of function, forming functional hemichannels at the cell surface that leak ATP into the extracellular medium. This aberrant paracrine signaling may alter the epidermal factors controlling keratinocyte proliferation and differentiation, contributing to the HED phenotype.
nodes.id: Aberrant Hemichannel Activity and ATP Release

evidence.reference_title: Connexin30 mutations responsible for hidrotic ectodermal dysplasia cause abnormal hemichannel activity.
evidence.snippet: we document a gain of function of G11R and A88V Cx30, which form functional hemichannels at the cell surface and, when expressed in HeLa cells, generate a leakage of ATP into the extracellular medium

nodes.description: Progressive hair loss beginning in childhood, ranging from sparse, fine, brittle hair to total alopecia. Eyebrows, eyelashes, and body hair may also be affected. Hair loss may be progressive, with some patients losing hair after puberty.

evidence.reference_title: Connexin30 mutations responsible for hidrotic ectodermal dysplasia cause abnormal hemichannel activity.
evidence.snippet: Clouston syndrome or hidrotic ectodermal dysplasia (HED) is a rare dominant genodermatosis characterized by palmoplantar hyperkeratosis, generalized alopecia and nail defects

evidence.reference_title: Phenotypic variability in gap junction syndromic skin disorders.
evidence.snippet: Hair is dry, fine, and brittle, and may be absent from the scalp, axillary, and pubic region

nodes.description: Dense follicular hyperkeratotic papules over the body surface, representing thickened skin with hyperkeratosis extending beyond the palms and soles.

evidence.reference_title: A recurrent mutation of GJB6 in a big Chinese family with Hidrotic ectodermal dysplasia.
evidence.snippet: The skin all over her body is thickened and hyperkeratosis with dense follicular hyperkeratotic papules

evidence.reference_title: A recurrent mutation of GJB6 in a big Chinese family with Hidrotic ectodermal dysplasia.
evidence.snippet: Four mutations (p.G11R, p.A88V, p.V37E and p.D50N) in gap junction beta 6 (GJB6) gene, which codes connexin30 protein, have been found to cause HED in different populations

evidence.reference_title: Novel mutations in GJB6 and GJB2 in Clouston syndrome.
evidence.snippet: Mutations in the GJB6 gene, which encodes the gap junction protein connexin 30, have been shown to cause this disorder. To date, four mutations of GJB6 have been found in patients with CS: G11R, V37E, D50N and A88V

evidence.reference_title: GJB6, of which mutations underlie Clouston syndrome, is a potential direct target gene of p63.
evidence.snippet: We identified a recurrent heterozygous mutation c.31G>C (p.Gly11Arg) in the GJB6 gene in the Lebanese-German family with Clouston syndrome

evidence.reference_title: GJB6 / Clouston syndrome (Definitive)
evidence.snippet: GJB6 | HGNC:4288 | Clouston syndrome | MONDO:0007510 | AD | Definitive

nodes.description: Connexin 30 is normally expressed in the inner root sheath and cortex of hair follicles. Mutations disrupt intercellular gap junction signaling in the hair follicle, leading to progressive alopecia. Hair is characteristically dry, fine, and brittle, and may be completely absent from the scalp, axillary, and pubic regions.

evidence.reference_title: GJB6, of which mutations underlie Clouston syndrome, is a potential direct target gene of p63.
evidence.snippet: Immunostaining in normal human skin sections demonstrated predominant expression of Cx30 in hair follicles, nails, and palmoplantar epidermis

evidence.reference_title: A recurrent mutation of GJB6 in a big Chinese family with Hidrotic ectodermal dysplasia.
evidence.snippet: Skin biopsy of trunk showed hyperkeratosis with normal distribution of eccrine and sebaceous gland, but absence of hair follicles

nodes.description: Darkening of skin, particularly over joints such as knuckles, elbows, and knees.

evidence.reference_title: Phenotypic variability in gap junction syndromic skin disorders.
evidence.snippet: The skin of his palms and soles was hyperkeratotic and mildly hyperpigmented on the joints

nodes.description: In the absence of wild-type Cx30, skin disease-associated Cx30 mutations show impaired trafficking to the plasma membrane, preventing formation of functional gap junctions. This loss-of-function mechanism reduces intercellular communication in keratinocytes, complementing the hemichannel gain-of-function mechanism.

evidence.reference_title: Functional studies of human skin disease- and deafness-associated connexin 30 mutations.
evidence.snippet: For all three of the skin disease-associated Cx30 mutations investigated, impaired trafficking of the protein to the plasma membrane was observed thus preventing the formation of functional Cx30 gap junctions

nodes.description: Dysfunctional gap junction communication in keratinocytes disrupts normal epidermal differentiation and desquamation. Reduced intercellular coupling combined with aberrant hemichannel-mediated ATP signaling leads to hyperkeratosis, particularly in the palmoplantar epidermis where Cx30 is normally highly expressed.

evidence.reference_title: Connexin30 mutations responsible for hidrotic ectodermal dysplasia cause abnormal hemichannel activity.
evidence.snippet: Such increased ATP levels might act as a paracrine messenger that, by altering the epidermal factors which control the proliferation and differentiation of keratinocytes, may play an important role in the pathophysiological processes leading to the HED phenotype

evidence.reference_title: Phenotypic variability in gap junction syndromic skin disorders.
evidence.snippet: Moderate to severe hyperkeratosis is often present, with reduced keratinocytes desquamation

nodes.description: Cx30 is expressed in inner-ear mesenchymal structures and supports gap-junction transport of potassium ions and small molecules. GJB6 variants can therefore plausibly produce hearing dysfunction in the minority of Clouston syndrome patients with hearing loss.

evidence.reference_title: A recurrent mutation of GJB6 in a big Chinese family with Hidrotic ectodermal dysplasia.
evidence.snippet: Cx30 encoded by GJB6 is an important member in connexin family which is highly expressed in epithelial, especially the palms of the hands and soles of the feet, hair follicles, nail beds and mesenchymal structures of the inner ear

nodes.description: Severe nail changes are the most consistent feature, present from birth or early childhood. Nails are thickened, hyperplastic, deformed with onycholysis, subungual hyperkeratosis, and yellow discoloration. Some patients show unusual pterygium formation and nail thinning. Cold-triggered nail pain has been reported in a pedigree with the G45A variant.

evidence.reference_title: Phenotypic variability in gap junction syndromic skin disorders.
evidence.snippet: nails are predominantly affected. They are thick, hyperplastic, and deformed with onycholysis

evidence.reference_title: A recurrent mutation of GJB6 in a big Chinese family with Hidrotic ectodermal dysplasia.
evidence.snippet: Physical examination revealed that her fingernails and toenails are atrophic, short, thickened and brittle with pterygium formation

evidence.reference_title: A novel variant in the GJB6 gene in a large Chinese family with a unique phenotype of Clouston syndrome.
evidence.snippet: We identified a new clinical phenotype involving all nail needling pain in all patients

nodes.description: Dysfunctional gap junctions in the nail matrix impair normal nail plate formation. Nail dystrophy is often the earliest and most consistent feature, with nails becoming thick, hyperplastic, deformed, and showing onycholysis. Some patients show unusual pterygium formation and nail thinning.

evidence.reference_title: Phenotypic variability in gap junction syndromic skin disorders.
evidence.snippet: nails are predominantly affected. They are thick, hyperplastic, and deformed with onycholysis

nodes.description: Diffuse thickening of the skin on palms and soles. A hallmark feature of hidrotic ectodermal dysplasia, though variable in severity and occasionally absent.

evidence.reference_title: Connexin30 mutations responsible for hidrotic ectodermal dysplasia cause abnormal hemichannel activity.
evidence.snippet: Clouston syndrome or hidrotic ectodermal dysplasia (HED) is a rare dominant genodermatosis characterized by palmoplantar hyperkeratosis, generalized alopecia and nail defects

evidence.reference_title: Phenotypic variability in gap junction syndromic skin disorders.
evidence.snippet: Clouston syndrome is characterized by nail dystrophy, hypotrichosis, and palmoplantar keratoderma

nodes.description: Hearing impairment has been reported in some patients with GJB6 mutations. While Cx30 is expressed in the inner ear, hearing loss is not a consistent feature of Clouston syndrome and may relate to specific mutations or additional genetic modifiers.

evidence.reference_title: A recurrent mutation of GJB6 in a big Chinese family with Hidrotic ectodermal dysplasia.
evidence.snippet: Only two patients have hearing disorders (the proband and her father)

nodes.description: Eyebrows may be sparse or absent in affected individuals, as part of the broader hypotrichosis phenotype.

evidence.reference_title: Phenotypic variability in gap junction syndromic skin disorders.
evidence.snippet: sparse eyebrows and eyelashes

nodes.description: Eyelashes may be sparse or absent in affected individuals.

evidence.reference_title: Phenotypic variability in gap junction syndromic skin disorders.
evidence.snippet: sparse eyebrows and eyelashes

nodes.description: Second most common mutation, c.263C>T. Introduces a hydrophobic residue in the transmembrane M2 domain. Recurrently reported in Chinese Han families.

evidence.reference_title: A recurrent mutation of GJB6 in a big Chinese family with Hidrotic ectodermal dysplasia.
evidence.snippet: Sequence analysis revealed she and other 23 family members all carry a recurrent missense mutation p.A88V (c.263C > T) in GJB6

nodes.description: Novel mutation identified in a Han Chinese pedigree.

evidence.reference_title: Novel mutations in GJB6 and GJB2 in Clouston syndrome.
evidence.snippet: We found a novel missense mutation, N14S, in GJB6 and the previously identified F191L mutation in GJB2 (Cx26) in a proband with CS in a Han Chinese pedigree

nodes.description: Fourth known pathogenic mutation in GJB6 causing HED.
Most common mutation worldwide. Results from c.31G>C substitution in the N-terminus of Cx30. Found in French-Canadian, Polish, Lebanese-German, and other populations.

evidence.reference_title: Phenotypic variability in gap junction syndromic skin disorders: experience from KID and Clouston syndromes' clinical diagnostics.
evidence.snippet: a family with Clouston syndrome (caused by p.Gly11Arg mutation in GJB6), who are the first reported patients of Polish origin suffering from this disorder

evidence.reference_title: GJB6, of which mutations underlie Clouston syndrome, is a potential direct target gene of p63.
evidence.snippet: We identified a recurrent heterozygous mutation c.31G>C (p.Gly11Arg) in the GJB6 gene in the Lebanese-German family with Clouston syndrome

nodes.description: Novel heterozygous missense variant (c.134G>C) identified in a large Chinese pedigree with a unique phenotype including cold-triggered nail pain. Classified as likely pathogenic by ACMG criteria.

evidence.reference_title: A novel variant in the GJB6 gene in a large Chinese family with a unique phenotype of Clouston syndrome.
evidence.snippet: We found a novel heterozygous missense variant (c.134G>C:p.G45A) for Clouston syndrome

nodes.description: Less common mutation identified in HED families.
